mcqs.question: What is a self-referential structure in C, as used for implementing a chain?

options.text: A structure that contains a pointer to itself or another structure of the same type
A structure that contains two data fields of the same type
A structure that is defined inside another structure
A structure that references all other structures in the program

mcqs.explanation: A self-referential structure contains a pointer to a variable of the same structure type, enabling node linking.

mcqs.question: Why is it crucial to check if malloc() returns NULL after allocating memory for a new node?

options.text: To prevent a segmentation fault when memory allocation fails
To determine the memory address of the newly allocated node
To ensure the node is properly initialized to zero
To check for syntax errors

mcqs.explanation: Checking for NULL prevents segmentation faults when memory allocation fails.

mcqs.question: What is the primary role of the head pointer in a singly linked list?

options.text: It stores the number of nodes in the list
It is the entry point that stores the address of the first node
It points to the last node for quick access
It keeps track of the middle node for balanced operations

mcqs.explanation: The head pointer is the entry point to the entire list, storing the address of the first node.

mcqs.question: Compare the time complexities of inserting a node at the beginning versus at the end of a singly linked list.

options.text: Both are O(1)
Beginning is O(n), end is O(1)
Beginning is O(1), end is O(n)
Both are O(n)

mcqs.explanation: Insertion at the beginning is O(1), while insertion at the end is O(n) due to traversal.

mcqs.question: In the three-pointer technique for reversing a linked list, which pointers are used?

options.text: head, tail, and temp pointers
prev, current, and next pointers
first, second, and third pointers
start, end, and middle pointers

mcqs.explanation: The three-pointer technique uses prev, current, and next pointers to reverse the list.

mcqs.question: What is a memory leak in the context of linked lists, and how can it be prevented?

options.text: Memory leak occurs when too much memory is allocated
Memory leak is when allocated memory becomes unreachable; prevented by calling free()
Memory leak happens when NULL pointers are used
Memory leak is when data is lost during traversal

mcqs.explanation: A memory leak occurs when allocated memory becomes unreachable; prevented by calling free().

mcqs.question: Which of the following is NOT an advantage of linked lists over arrays?

options.text: Dynamic size that grows at runtime
O(1) insertion at the beginning
O(1) random access by index
No memory wastage due to pre-allocation

mcqs.explanation: Linked lists do not support O(1) random access by index.

mcqs.question: What is the standard traversal pattern used to visit each node in a linked list exactly once?

options.text: Start at the tail, move backward until head is reached
Start at the head, process node, move to next until NULL is encountered
Start at a random position and traverse in both directions
Use recursion only, never iterative approach

mcqs.explanation: The standard traversal pattern starts at the head and moves to the next node until NULL is encountered.
